Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have a study routine. I'd tell myself I would study after my extracurricular activities, but then I'd get home exhausted and just scroll on my phone instead. Sometimes I'd have these bursts where I'd try to cram everything at once, usually late at night when the anxiety got too overwhelming to ignore. My room is a mess of downloaded PDFs I printed at the library and notes I started but never finished.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. I'd go days without touching anything course-related, then panic when I realized a deadline was close. With my extracurriculars taking up time and honestly just feeling scared about everything, I couldn't stick to any schedule. Some weeks I'd study maybe once, other weeks I'd have these frantic sessions at 2am trying to catch up.
Q3: Usually it was fear that made me log in. I'd get this wave of panic thinking about how far behind I was, or I'd see a notification about a deadline and my heart would start racing. Sometimes I'd log in just to see how bad things were, which usually made me feel worse. Occasionally a classmate would mention something about the course and I'd realize I had no idea what was happening.
Q4: Not really, no. I never got to that point where it felt automatic or normal. Every time I logged in felt like this dreaded thing I had to force myself to do. I kept telling myself I'd make it a habit starting Monday, or after this event, but it never stuck. The anxiety around it just made me avoid it more.
Q5: The video lectures were probably the most useful because I'm a visual learner and I could pause and rewind when I got confused. I liked being able to watch them at my own pace, even though I rarely finished them completely. The discussion posts were sometimes helpful too because I could see how other students understood things, which made me feel less alone in being confused.
Q6: I basically ignored the readings and a lot of the supplementary materials. They felt overwhelming and I didn't have money to print everything, so I'd download them and then never open them again. The practice quizzes I avoided because I was scared of seeing how little I actually knew. It was easier to just not look than to confirm how behind I was.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see immediate results or if it directly connected to an assignment I was panicking about. If something felt too abstract or like it wouldn't help my grade right away, I couldn't justify spending my limited energy on it. With everything else going on and my resources being tight, I had to be really selective, even if that meant cutting corners.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford. When you're barely keeping up with required stuff and constantly anxious, optional things feel like they're for students who have their lives together. Required activities at least had deadlines that forced me to act, even if I only partially completed them. Optional stuff just fell off my radar completely.
Q9: Yeah, a lot actually. I'd download lecture slides and videos when I had wifi at the library because my internet at home isn't reliable. I'd study from screenshots on my phone during bus rides or between my extracurricular activities. Sometimes I'd borrow a classmate's notes and photograph them. A lot of my studying happened in these fragmented, invisible ways.
Q10: There were times I'd spend hours going through printed materials or my phone screenshots, but none of that would show up anywhere. I'd also think about course concepts during my other activities, like trying to connect things in my head. Some of my best understanding came from explaining things to myself while walking, but obviously that's not tracked anywhere.
Q11: Definitely. I'd watch part of a video, download the slides, then review those slides offline multiple times. My internet situation meant I had to work this way. I'd access something once to grab what I needed, then study it in bits and pieces over days without ever logging back in. The LMS probably thought I barely looked at things I actually spent hours on.
Q12: Around midterms everything fell apart. My anxiety spiked so high I could barely function, and I had commitments with my extracurricular group that I couldn't back out of. I started having these moments where I'd open the LMS, feel completely overwhelmed by how much I'd missed, and just close it immediately. The CBT sessions helped a little but I was already so far behind.
Q13: All of those things, constantly. The workload felt impossible when I was already stretched thin financially and emotionally. Confusion made me avoid things because I was scared of feeling stupid. Fatigue from my activities and stress meant I'd sit in front of my laptop and just zone out. My brain felt like it was running on empty most of the time.
Q14: So many times. I'd have the LMS open and just stare at it, not really processing anything. Or I'd click through videos while my mind was somewhere else, worrying about money or upcoming deadlines or just feeling this vague dread. My eyes were on the screen but nothing was actually going in. It was like going through the motions.
Q15: I postponed everything important, honestly. The bigger and more important something was, the more scared I felt about it, so the more I avoided it. I knew assignments were important but the fear of failing them made me put them off until the last possible moment, or sometimes past it. It's like my brain couldn't handle the pressure so it just shut down.
Q16: Deadlines were the only thing that made me do anything, but they also made everything worse. I'd ignore things until a deadline was screaming at me, then scramble in a panic. Sometimes I'd miss deadlines entirely because the anxiety paralyzed me. The deadline pressure was both the only thing motivating me and the thing destroying my mental health.
Q17: I honestly avoided looking at feedback a lot of the time. When I did see it, if it was negative, it would send me into a spiral for days. Good feedback was rare and felt like a fluke. I know feedback is supposed to help you improve, but for me it mostly just confirmed my fears that I wasn't good enough for this.
Q18: The structure felt really rigid and unforgiving. Missing one thing made it hard to understand the next thing, so once I fell behind, it snowballed. There wasn't much flexibility for someone like me who was struggling with attendance and keeping up. It felt like the course was designed for students who could show up consistently and stay on track.
Q19: The weekly modules made it clear what I was supposed to do, which was helpful in theory. But the amount of content each week was overwhelming given everything else in my life. Having discussion posts helped me feel connected sometimes, but the strict deadlines made everything feel high-stakes and scary. More flexibility would have helped me a lot.
Q20: That makes sense to me. Sometimes you're logging in and clicking around trying to understand what's happening, but you're too confused or scared to actually submit anything. Or you're checking things repeatedly because you're anxious, not because you're being productive. High activity can just mean high anxiety without actual output.
Q21: I guess some people are just more efficient or they study in ways that don't require the LMS. Maybe they have better support systems or resources outside of class. For me, my low activity reflected genuine disengagement, but I can see how someone could learn effectively offline and just use the LMS minimally for submissions.
Q22: Course logs can show when you clicked on things and for how long, but they can't show whether you actually understood anything or how you were feeling. They miss all the offline studying, the mental state behind the clicks, the circumstances in someone's life. They see the surface but not what's underneath, which for someone like me is a lot of fear and struggle.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and feeling like you can succeed at it. It means showing up mentally, not just physically clicking through things. For me, true engagement would require feeling safe enough to try without being paralyzed by fear of failure. I wanted to be engaged but my anxiety kept getting in the way.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how scared I was all the time, or how much I actually wanted to do better but couldn't make myself function. It wouldn't show the hours I spent anxious about the course without actually being able to work on it. It wouldn't show my financial stress, my offline studying attempts, or how the CBT sessions were the only thing keeping me from completely giving up.
